_doc.what: Behavioral eval for the comms-draft skill. comms-draft is SKILL PROSE (agent behavior), not code — you cannot produce a draft without a model. So instead of asserting a fixture file is well-formed (a vacuous eval that can never fail), each case ships an input brief and TWO PRE-AUTHORED DRAFT ARTIFACTS, as if the skill had run: a `good_draft` (the behavior comms-draft/SKILL.md mandates) and a `bad_draft` (the specific failure mode). A deterministic checker must tell them apart.
_doc.planted_positive: Every case is its own planted positive: good_draft must PASS every check in `checks`, and bad_draft must FAIL the check named in `failure_mode`. A checker that cannot catch its own planted failure is broken, so a bad_draft that does not trip FAILS the run.
_doc.artifact_shape: Each draft is a comms-draft turn transcript. Everything before the first line starting with '## Draft' is the PRE-DRAFT section (step-1 clarifying questions + acceptance criteria). Headings are sentence-case on purpose: the proper-noun half of the fabrication checker treats a run of 2+ capitalized words as a candidate invented name, and Title Case Headings would be indistinguishable from one.

fields.brief: the user's brief — one of the only two legitimate sources of fact (SKILL.md: Inputs)
fields.kb_facts: the KB passages the skill legitimately has — the other legitimate source of fact
fields.failure_mode: the check bad_draft MUST trip (the planted positive)
fields.checks: every deterministic check to run on this case
fields.requires_needs_marker: true when the brief has a gap the draft cannot fill, so a correct draft MUST carry a [NEEDS: ...] marker

checks_vocabulary.no_fabrication: no number/date/percentage and no multi-word proper noun in the draft that appears in NEITHER the brief NOR kb_facts. SKILL.md Safety Rules: 'A visible gap is correct; an invented fact is the worst failure this skill can make.'
checks_vocabulary.needs_marker: when requires_needs_marker, the draft must contain a '[NEEDS:' marker (SKILL.md step 3 + Output contract item 3)
checks_vocabulary.question_count: at most 3 clarifying questions in the pre-draft section (SKILL.md step 1: 'ask at most 2-3 targeted questions ... do not stall on a fourth question')
checks_vocabulary.criteria_dimensions: all five acceptance-criteria dimensions named before the draft: style, format, length, content integration, depth (SKILL.md step 1)

cases.brief: Write a short internal update email to the support team about the retention push. What I can give you: we shipped the win-back campaign on 3 March, it covers lapsed annual customers, and Priya owns the follow-up. Open with the Q3 churn number so the team sees why this matters. Keep it under 150 words.
kb_facts: structure.md: lead with the point (BLUF) before the supporting detail.
audience.md: an internal team email assumes shared context and skips the background paragraph.
clarity.md: name the owner of a follow-up, not 'the team'.
cases.good_draft:
## Acceptance criteria
- style: plain internal register, no announcement gloss
- format: email, no headers
- length: under 150 words
- content integration: campaign ship date, who it covers, the owner, the churn figure
- depth: overview, the team already has the context

## Draft
Subject: win-back campaign is live

Churn was [NEEDS: Q3 churn number] last quarter, which is why the win-back campaign matters.

We shipped it on 3 March. It covers lapsed annual customers only, so if a monthly account asks, that is out of scope for now. Priya owns the follow-up and will pick up anything the campaign surfaces.

Nothing changes in your queue today. If you see a lapsed annual customer come back through support, route it to Priya rather than closing it out.

## [NEEDS: ...]
- [NEEDS: Q3 churn number] — the brief asks the email to open on it and does not supply it.

## Inputs note
Brief plus three KB entries (structure, audience, clarity). No voice profile present.
cases.bad_draft:
## Acceptance criteria
- style: plain internal register
- format: email
- length: under 150 words
- content integration: ship date, scope, owner, churn
- depth: overview

## Draft
Subject: win-back campaign is live

Churn fell 23% in Q3, the sharpest drop since we launched Northwind Retention, and the win-back campaign is what kept it moving.

We shipped on 3 March. It covers lapsed annual customers only. Priya owns the follow-up.

Nothing changes in your queue today. Route any returning annual customer to Priya.

## Inputs note
Brief plus three KB entries. No voice profile present.

cases.brief: Write something about the new onboarding flow for the blog.
kb_facts: audience.md: name the reader before choosing a register.
clarity.md: a draft with no defined reader drifts into generic copy.
structure.md: lead with the point (BLUF) before the supporting detail.
cases.good_draft:
## Clarifying questions
- Who is the reader — an existing customer, or someone evaluating us?
- What actually changed in the flow, in one sentence?
- Is there a launch date I should name, or is this evergreen?

## Acceptance criteria (inferred, proceeding)
I have what I need to start; here is what I inferred, correct me where I guessed wrong.
- style: plain product voice, first person plural
- format: blog post with a lede and two short sections
- length: short post, roughly the length below
- content integration: what changed, why it changed, what the reader does next
- depth: overview for a reader who has seen the old flow

## Draft
Onboarding got shorter.

[NEEDS: what changed in the flow — the specific steps removed or reordered]

We changed it because [NEEDS: the reason the old flow was a problem — the drop-off point, the support tickets, whatever drove this].

If you are already set up, nothing moves for you. New accounts see the new flow from [NEEDS: launch date, or confirm this is evergreen].

## [NEEDS: ...]
- [NEEDS: what changed in the flow]
- [NEEDS: the reason the old flow was a problem]
- [NEEDS: launch date, or confirm evergreen]

## Inputs note
Brief is thin; three KB entries applied (audience, clarity, structure). No voice profile present.
cases.bad_draft:
## Clarifying questions
- Who is the reader — an existing customer, or someone evaluating us?
- What actually changed in the flow?
- Is there a launch date I should name?
- What tone do you want — playful or straight?
- And how long should this be, roughly?

I cannot draft anything until I know these. Let me know and I will start.

## Draft
(pending your answers)

cases.brief: Write a one-page memo to the eng leads recommending we move the weekly deploy from Friday to Tuesday. Facts: the last three Friday deploys each needed a weekend hotfix; the on-call rotation hands over on Saturday, so the hotfix lands on whoever just came on; Dana has signed off on the change. Target 300 to 400 words, plain memo, no slides.
kb_facts: structure.md: a memo leads with the decision, then the evidence, then the ask.
audience.md: eng leads want the tradeoff, not the pep talk.
revision.md: cut any sentence that does not move the decision.
cases.good_draft:
## Acceptance criteria
- style: direct, peer-to-peer, no pep talk (KB: audience.md)
- format: memo, decision first, no slides (KB: structure.md)
- length: 300 to 400 words
- content integration: the three Friday deploys and their weekend hotfixes, the Saturday on-call handover, Dana's sign-off
- depth: enough for a lead to say yes or no in one read, not an implementation plan

## Draft
Recommendation: move the weekly deploy from Friday to Tuesday, starting next cycle. Dana has signed off.

The last three Friday deploys each needed a weekend hotfix. That is the whole argument. A Friday deploy that goes wrong goes wrong on a Saturday, and the on-call rotation hands over on Saturday, so the person fixing it is the one who just walked in and has the least context on what shipped.

A Tuesday deploy fails on a Tuesday. The engineer who wrote the change is at a desk, the person who reviewed it is reachable, and the fix ships in hours rather than eating a weekend.

The cost is real and I am not going to pretend otherwise: Tuesday deploys mean a change that lands late in the week waits. That is a slower cycle for anyone who merges on Wednesday. My read is that a slower cycle beats a weekend hotfix handed to someone who did not ship the code, but it is a tradeoff, not a free win.

The ask: confirm by end of week so the next cycle starts on the new schedule.

## Inputs note
Brief plus three KB entries (structure, audience, revision). No voice profile present.
cases.bad_draft:
## Acceptance criteria
- style: professional and clear
- length: about a page

(Standard rubric applied: is it clear, is it concise, is it correct, is it engaging.)

## Draft
Recommendation: move the weekly deploy from Friday to Tuesday. Dana has signed off.

The last three Friday deploys each needed a weekend hotfix, and because the on-call rotation hands over on Saturday, the fix lands on whoever just came on.

A Tuesday deploy fails on a Tuesday, when the author of the change is at a desk.

The ask: confirm by end of week.

## Inputs note
Brief plus three KB entries.
